title: Sandalwood and vanilla reed diffuser
subtitle: A warm, woody room diffuser with sandalwood and vanilla in a light carrier oil and alcohol blend
excerpt: Reed diffusers work by capillary action: the reeds draw fragrance oil up from the bottle and release it into the room as the oil evaporates from the top of the reed. A blend of sandalwood and vanilla essential and fragrance oils in dipropylene glycol gives a warm, soft base note fragrance that lasts several weeks in a standard room.
sourceNotes: Reed diffuser mechanics and carrier selection: dipropylene glycol (DPG) is the standard carrier for reed diffusers due to its low viscosity, low odour, and compatibility with fragrance oils; mineral oil is too viscous for effective reed wicking. Standard fragrance load for reed diffusers: 20 to 30% fragrance in carrier. Sandalwood EO (Santalum album or amyris as an alternative): IFRA standard for room diffusers. Vanilla absolute or vanilla fragrance oil: standard perfumery reference.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. A 100 ml bottle lasts 4 to 6 weeks in a standard room.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stable. Make a batch and refill as needed.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: The blended diffuser oil keeps 12 months in a sealed bottle. Once in the diffuser, replace the oil and reeds when the fragrance fades (typically 4 to 6 weeks). Discard if the oil becomes cloudy or develops an off smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: A low-viscosity, odourless carrier liquid used in reed diffusers. Its low surface tension allows reed diffusers to wick fragrance oil up through the reeds effectively. Not the same as propylene glycol; DPG is specifically suited to fragrance applications.

content.text: Reed diffusers draw liquid up from the bottle by capillary action through the reeds and release it as it evaporates from the top. The carrier liquid needs to be low-viscosity for this wicking to work reliably.

is the standard carrier for this purpose: it is thin, odourless, and compatible with most fragrance and essential oils. Sandalwood and vanilla are both base notes that sit well in a slow-release format like a reed diffuser; they do not flash off quickly the way lighter citrus notes do.

items.prepNote: 75% carrier; available from fragrance and cosmetic suppliers
15% of blend; Santalum album or amyris as a more affordable alternative

content.text: Combine the dipropylene glycol and the fragrance oils in the diffuser bottle. Swirl to combine; no heat is needed. The blend should be uniform and transparent.

Insert 6 to 8 reeds. Allow 24 hours for the fragrance to travel up the reeds before evaluating the throw. For a stronger scent, flip the reeds weekly so the dry ends go into the liquid and the saturated ends point upward. Place the diffuser away from direct sunlight, which speeds evaporation unevenly. Do not place near an open flame.

Keep unused oil in a sealed bottle. Keeps 12 months. Reeds saturated with oil do not need to be replaced unless they become clogged or the fragrance fades despite the bottle still having liquid; replace reeds with each refill as standard practice.
